global_crash.headline: Markets in Free Fall: {{asset_class}} Plunge {{pct_drop}}% in Worst Session Since {{historical_ref}}
global_crash.body: Global markets experienced severe selling pressure as {{catalyst}}. {{asset_class}} indices dropped {{pct_drop}}% in a single session, echoing the turmoil seen during {{historical_ref}}.
global_crash.beginner_explainer: A market crash means prices fall very fast. This is similar to what happened during {{historical_ref}} — investors panic and sell everything at once.

global_crash.headline: Wall Street Rocked: Dow Drops Over {{points}} Points Amid {{catalyst}}
global_crash.body: US equity markets suffered their sharpest decline in {{timeframe}} as {{catalyst}} rattled investor confidence. The Dow Jones fell {{points}} points, dragging global indices lower.
global_crash.beginner_explainer: The Dow Jones is a famous index that tracks 30 big US companies. A big drop like this means investors are very worried about the economy.

global_crash.headline: Global Sell-Off: Investors Flee Risk Assets as {{catalyst}} Deepens
global_crash.body: A wave of risk-aversion swept markets worldwide. Equities, corporate bonds, and crypto all fell sharply while gold and government bonds rallied as investors sought safety.
global_crash.beginner_explainer: When people get scared about the economy, they sell risky investments like stocks and crypto and buy safe ones like gold and government bonds.

banking_panic.headline: Banking Turmoil: {{bank_name}} Faces Collapse as Depositors Rush for Exits
banking_panic.body: Confidence in {{bank_name}} evaporated overnight as reports of {{trigger}} sparked a depositor run. Regulators scrambled to contain contagion fears across the financial system.
banking_panic.beginner_explainer: A bank run happens when everyone tries to withdraw their money at the same time. Banks don't keep all deposits on hand, so they can run out of cash very quickly.

banking_panic.headline: Regulators Step In: Emergency Measures to Prevent Banking Contagion
banking_panic.body: Financial authorities announced emergency backstop facilities after {{trigger}}. The measures aim to reassure depositors and prevent panic from spreading to otherwise healthy banks.
banking_panic.beginner_explainer: When one bank fails, people worry their bank might be next. Regulators step in with emergency support to stop the fear from spreading — like a firebreak in a forest fire.

banking_panic.headline: Interbank Lending Freezes as Trust Evaporates in Financial System
banking_panic.body: Banks halted lending to each other amid uncertainty about counterparty exposures following {{trigger}}. Short-term funding markets seized up, echoing the credit freeze of {{historical_ref}}.
banking_panic.beginner_explainer: Banks normally lend money to each other every day. When they stop — because they don't trust each other — the whole financial system can grind to a halt.

war_escalation.headline: Geopolitical Crisis: {{conflict}} Sends Oil Past ${{oil_price}} and Stocks Tumbling
war_escalation.body: Markets reacted sharply to the escalation of {{conflict}}. Crude oil spiked to ${{oil_price}} per barrel on supply fears while equity markets sold off on rising uncertainty.
war_escalation.beginner_explainer: Wars disrupt trade routes and energy supplies. When a conflict involves a major oil-producing region, fuel prices jump and stock markets fall on uncertainty.

war_escalation.headline: Safe Havens Surge: Gold and Treasuries Rally as {{conflict}} Intensifies
war_escalation.body: Investors poured into safe-haven assets as {{conflict}} intensified. Gold rose {{gold_chg}}% and Treasury yields fell sharply as capital rotated out of equities.
war_escalation.beginner_explainer: During wars, investors move money into 'safe' assets — gold and government bonds — because they're less likely to lose value during a crisis.

war_escalation.headline: Defence Stocks Rally While Broader Market Slides on {{conflict}} Fears
war_escalation.body: The defence sector outperformed as {{conflict}} raised expectations of increased military spending. The broader market, however, fell on concerns about supply-chain disruption and energy costs.
war_escalation.beginner_explainer: Companies that make military equipment often see their stock prices rise during conflicts, while most other companies suffer from higher costs and uncertainty.

recession_onset.headline: It's Official: Economy Enters Recession After {{quarters}} Quarters of Contraction
recession_onset.body: Economic data confirmed what markets had feared — the economy has officially entered a recession. GDP contracted for {{quarters}} consecutive quarters as consumer spending and business investment weakened.
recession_onset.beginner_explainer: A recession means the economy is shrinking. Companies make less money, some people lose their jobs, and everyone becomes more careful with spending.

recession_onset.headline: Recession Fears Confirmed: Unemployment Rises as Growth Stalls
recession_onset.body: The latest employment report showed unemployment rising to {{unemployment_rate}}%, reinforcing recession concerns. Defensive sectors outperformed while cyclicals lagged sharply.
recession_onset.beginner_explainer: When more people lose their jobs, they spend less money, which means companies earn less — it's a negative cycle that defines recessions.

recession_onset.headline: Markets Brace: Recession Signals Flash Red Across the Dashboard
recession_onset.body: The yield curve inversion, falling leading indicators, and weakening PMI data all point to a recession underway. Equity markets priced in further earnings downgrades.
recession_onset.beginner_explainer: Economists use many signals to predict recessions. When most of them turn negative at once, it's a strong warning that the economy is in trouble.

emergency_policy_intervention.headline: Emergency Move: Central Bank Cuts Rates by {{bps}}bps in Unscheduled Decision
emergency_policy_intervention.body: In a rare unscheduled meeting, the central bank slashed interest rates by {{bps}} basis points to combat {{trigger}}. Markets initially rallied but volatility remained elevated.
emergency_policy_intervention.beginner_explainer: Central banks normally change interest rates at scheduled meetings. An emergency cut outside that schedule means they think the situation is very serious.

emergency_policy_intervention.headline: Government Announces ${{bailout_size}} Bailout Package for {{sector}}
emergency_policy_intervention.body: Authorities unveiled a {{bailout_size}} billion dollar rescue package for the {{sector}} sector after {{trigger}} threatened systemic stability. The intervention aims to restore confidence.
emergency_policy_intervention.beginner_explainer: A bailout is when the government uses taxpayer money to save companies or banks that are about to fail, to prevent a bigger economic disaster.

emergency_policy_intervention.headline: Unprecedented: Central Bank Launches Emergency Asset Purchase Programme
emergency_policy_intervention.body: The central bank announced it would begin purchasing {{asset_type}} to stabilise markets following {{trigger}}. The open-ended programme marks a historic expansion of monetary policy tools.
emergency_policy_intervention.beginner_explainer: Sometimes cutting interest rates isn't enough. The central bank starts buying bonds and other assets directly to pump money into the economy — this is called quantitative easing.

major_commodity_shock.headline: Oil Shock: Crude Surges {{pct_change}}% as {{catalyst}} Disrupts Supply
major_commodity_shock.body: Crude oil prices spiked {{pct_change}}% after {{catalyst}} disrupted a significant portion of global supply. Energy-importing nations face rising costs and inflation pressure.
major_commodity_shock.beginner_explainer: Oil is used in almost everything — fuel, plastics, shipping. When its price jumps suddenly, costs go up for everyone and the economy slows down.

major_commodity_shock.headline: Energy Crisis: Governments Tap Strategic Reserves as Prices Soar
major_commodity_shock.body: Multiple governments announced releases from strategic petroleum reserves as {{catalyst}} pushed energy prices to multi-year highs. The coordinated response aims to cool markets.
major_commodity_shock.beginner_explainer: Countries keep emergency oil reserves for crises. When they start using them, it means the situation is serious — like breaking the glass on a fire extinguisher.

major_commodity_shock.headline: Commodity Rout: {{commodity}} Prices Collapse {{pct_change}}% on {{catalyst}}
major_commodity_shock.body: {{commodity}} prices plunged {{pct_change}}% as {{catalyst}} upended market expectations. Producers face severe margin pressure while consumers benefit from falling input costs.
major_commodity_shock.beginner_explainer: Commodity prices can fall just as sharply as they rise. A sudden drop helps buyers (like factories) but hurts producers (like mining companies).

hawkish_rate_hike.headline: Fed Hikes Rates {{bps}}bps — Signals More to Come
hawkish_rate_hike.body: The Federal Reserve raised interest rates by {{bps}} basis points and signalled further tightening ahead. Chair noted that inflation remains 'unacceptably high'.
hawkish_rate_hike.beginner_explainer: Raising interest rates is the main tool central banks use to fight inflation. Higher rates make borrowing more expensive, which slows spending and price increases.

hawkish_rate_hike.headline: Rate Hike Hits Markets: Stocks Fall, Bond Yields Rise
hawkish_rate_hike.body: Equity markets declined and bond yields jumped after the latest rate hike. Growth stocks were hit hardest as higher rates reduce the present value of future earnings.
hawkish_rate_hike.beginner_explainer: When rates go up, stocks tend to fall — especially fast-growing tech companies — because higher rates make future profits worth less in today's dollars.

dovish_rate_cut.headline: Central Bank Cuts Rates by {{bps}}bps as Growth Slows
dovish_rate_cut.body: The central bank lowered its benchmark rate by {{bps}} basis points, citing weakening economic data. Markets rallied on expectations of easier financial conditions.
dovish_rate_cut.beginner_explainer: Cutting rates makes borrowing cheaper, which helps businesses invest and people buy homes. Markets usually go up because it's a sign the central bank is supporting the economy.

dovish_rate_cut.headline: Relief Rally: Stocks Surge After Rate Cut Signals Economic Support
dovish_rate_cut.body: Equity markets jumped broadly after the rate cut. Real estate and utility sectors led gains as lower rates reduce financing costs and boost dividend appeal.
dovish_rate_cut.beginner_explainer: Lower interest rates are usually good for stocks. Companies can borrow more cheaply, and savings accounts pay less — so more money flows into the stock market.

inflation_surprise.headline: Inflation Shock: CPI Comes in at {{cpi_pct}}% — Above All Forecasts
inflation_surprise.body: Consumer prices rose {{cpi_pct}}% year-over-year, significantly above the consensus forecast of {{forecast_pct}}%. Markets sold off as investors braced for a more aggressive central bank response.
inflation_surprise.beginner_explainer: CPI measures how fast prices are rising. When it's higher than expected, it usually means the central bank will raise rates more aggressively, which is bad for stocks and bonds.

inflation_surprise.headline: Inflation Cools: CPI Drops to {{cpi_pct}}% — Best Print in {{months}} Months
inflation_surprise.body: Inflation eased more than expected, with CPI falling to {{cpi_pct}}% year-over-year. Markets rallied on hopes that the rate-hiking cycle may be nearing its end.
inflation_surprise.beginner_explainer: When inflation drops, it's good news for investors. It means the central bank might stop raising rates soon, which helps stock and bond prices recover.

policy_guidance_shift.headline: Fed Shifts Tone: 'Data-Dependent' Replaces Forward Guidance
policy_guidance_shift.body: The central bank dropped its explicit forward guidance, stating future decisions will be 'data-dependent'. Markets interpreted the shift as a potential pause in the tightening cycle.
policy_guidance_shift.beginner_explainer: Central banks sometimes tell you what they plan to do next. When they stop giving clear guidance, it means they're unsure — and markets have to guess, which creates volatility.

policy_guidance_shift.headline: Hawkish Pivot: Central Bank Warns Rates May Stay 'Higher for Longer'
policy_guidance_shift.body: Policymakers surprised markets by signalling rates will remain elevated longer than previously expected. Bond yields rose and rate-sensitive sectors underperformed.
policy_guidance_shift.beginner_explainer: If the central bank says rates will stay high for a long time, it means borrowing stays expensive. That's tough for companies with lots of debt and for people wanting mortgages.

oil_price_spike.headline: Oil Jumps {{pct_change}}% on {{catalyst}} — Energy Stocks Rally
oil_price_spike.body: Crude prices surged {{pct_change}}% after {{catalyst}}. Energy producers rallied while airlines, shipping, and consumer discretionary stocks fell on higher input costs.
oil_price_spike.beginner_explainer: When oil prices jump, companies that produce oil make more money, but companies that use a lot of fuel — like airlines — make less because their costs go up.

oil_price_spike.headline: OPEC+ Surprises With Production Cut — Crude Rallies
oil_price_spike.body: OPEC+ announced an unexpected production cut of {{cut_mbpd}} million barrels per day. Crude prices jumped as the supply reduction tightens global markets.
oil_price_spike.beginner_explainer: OPEC is a group of oil-producing countries. When they cut production, there's less oil available, so the price goes up — like when a popular toy is in short supply.

bond_yield_jump.headline: Yields Spike: {{benchmark}} Hits {{yield_pct}}% — Highest in {{years}} Years
bond_yield_jump.body: The {{benchmark}} yield rose sharply to {{yield_pct}}%, the highest level in {{years}} years. The move tightened financial conditions and pressured equity valuations.
bond_yield_jump.beginner_explainer: Bond yields are basically the interest rate the government pays to borrow money. When they go up fast, it makes all other borrowing more expensive too.

bond_yield_jump.headline: Bond Sell-Off Deepens: Investors Demand Higher Returns
bond_yield_jump.body: Government bond prices fell further as investors demanded higher yields to compensate for inflation risk and rising government debt levels.
bond_yield_jump.beginner_explainer: When bond prices fall, yields rise — they move in opposite directions. Rising yields mean investors are worried about inflation or government spending.

safe_haven_rotation.headline: Flight to Safety: Gold Rises {{gold_chg}}% as Uncertainty Mounts
safe_haven_rotation.body: Gold rallied {{gold_chg}}% as investors rotated out of risk assets amid {{catalyst}}. The move reflects elevated demand for portfolio hedges in uncertain times.
safe_haven_rotation.beginner_explainer: Gold is seen as a safe place to put your money when things are uncertain. It's been valuable for thousands of years, so people trust it during crises.

safe_haven_rotation.headline: Treasuries Rally as Investors Seek Shelter From Market Volatility
safe_haven_rotation.body: US Treasury prices surged, pushing yields lower, as investors fled risk assets. The classic flight-to-quality trade reflects deep uncertainty about the economic outlook.
safe_haven_rotation.beginner_explainer: Government bonds from stable countries like the US are considered very safe. When investors are scared, they buy these bonds, pushing their prices up.

daily_macro_watch.headline: Markets Steady as {{data_release}} Comes in Near Expectations
daily_macro_watch.body: {{data_release}} data was broadly in line with forecasts, providing little reason for significant market moves. Indices traded in a narrow range.
daily_macro_watch.beginner_explainer: When economic data matches what people expected, markets tend to be calm. No surprises means no reason to buy or sell aggressively.

daily_macro_watch.headline: Mixed Signals: Jobs Strong but Manufacturing Slows
daily_macro_watch.body: The latest jobs report showed solid hiring, but manufacturing PMI data pointed to a slowdown. Markets digested the conflicting signals with modest moves.
daily_macro_watch.beginner_explainer: Sometimes different parts of the economy send different signals. That's normal — the economy is complex and not everything moves in the same direction.

sector_rotation.headline: Tech Leads as Investors Rotate From Defensives
sector_rotation.body: Technology stocks outperformed as improving risk appetite drove capital away from defensive sectors like utilities and consumer staples.
sector_rotation.beginner_explainer: Investors move money between different types of stocks depending on their confidence. When they feel optimistic, they buy riskier tech stocks.

earnings_tone.headline: Earnings Season: Solid Results but Cautious Guidance
earnings_tone.body: Companies mostly beat earnings expectations, but forward guidance was cautious. Executives cited {{concern}} as a headwind for coming quarters.
earnings_tone.beginner_explainer: Earnings season is when companies report how much money they made. Even good results can worry investors if companies say the future looks tough.

market_chatter.headline: Quiet Day on Wall Street — Volumes Below Average
market_chatter.body: Markets drifted sideways on below-average trading volume. With no major catalysts on the calendar, participants await next week's {{upcoming_event}}.
market_chatter.beginner_explainer: Not every day has big news. Sometimes markets are quiet and prices barely move. Traders often wait for important events before making big bets.

market_chatter.headline: Analysts Debate: Is the Rally Sustainable?
market_chatter.body: Wall Street strategists offered mixed views on the market's recent gains. Bulls point to strong earnings; bears warn of stretched valuations.
market_chatter.beginner_explainer: Analysts are people who study the market for a living. They often disagree — that's what makes markets work. Some think prices will keep rising (bulls) and others think they'll fall (bears).
